chunks.title: Preventive Maintenance Schedule for Commercial Trucks
chunks.content: Structured preventive maintenance prevents costly breakdowns and extends vehicle life. Oil and oil filter change every 10,000-15,000 km is essential for Indian conditions. Brake inspection and adjustment every 30,000 km—brake shoes may need replacement. Clutch plate replacement typically at 80,000-1,00,000 km depending on driving conditions. Air filter replacement every 20,000-30,000 km, more frequently in dusty areas. Coolant flush every 50,000 km. Grease suspension and steering components every 5,000-10,000 km. Document all maintenance in vehicle history; OEM schedules may vary—consult manufacturer manuals for specific models.

chunks.title: Maintenance Cost Benchmarks in Indian Market
chunks.content: Understanding repair cost benchmarks helps budget and negotiate. Engine overhaul ranges Rs 80,000-2,00,000 depending on truck size and extent of work. Brake shoes: Rs 2,000-5,000 per set. Clutch plate: Rs 8,000-15,000 for medium trucks. Alternator: Rs 5,000-12,000. Turbocharger: Rs 25,000-60,000. Radiator repair: Rs 3,000-8,000. Fuel injectors: Rs 15,000-40,000 per set. Costs vary by OEM vs aftermarket, region, and workshop. Metro cities command 15-25% premium over tier-2 locations. Maintain a cost database for fleet benchmarking and variance analysis.

chunks.title: Tyre Management and Life Expectancy
chunks.content: Proper tyre management significantly impacts operating costs. Good quality truck tyres last 80,000-1,00,000 km with proper maintenance. Retreading extends life by 40-60% at 20-30% of new tyre cost—ideal for fleet economics. Implement rotation schedule: move front tyres to rear every 25,000-30,000 km to equalize wear. Maintain correct pressure—under-inflation reduces life by 25-30%. Avoid overload which causes premature failure. Inspect for cuts, bulges, and uneven wear monthly. Store spare tyres away from direct sunlight. Track tyre life by position for data-driven replacement decisions.

chunks.title: Indian Tyre Brands and Positioning
chunks.content: Indian truck tyre market offers options across price segments. MRF is premium positioning with strong OEM fitment and 90,000+ km life expectancy. Apollo is mid-premium with good balance of price and performance. CEAT offers mid-segment options popular with fleet operators. JK Tyre provides budget-to-mid range with wide availability. Bridgestone and Michelin are premium imports for specialized applications. Choose based on route profile: highway-heavy fleets benefit from premium tyres; mixed use may suit mid-segment. Consider total cost of ownership—premium tyres often have lower cost per km despite higher upfront price.

chunks.title: Workshop Selection: Authorized vs Independent
chunks.content: Workshop choice affects cost, quality, and warranty. Authorized dealer workshops use OEM parts and trained technicians—essential during warranty period. Labor rates at authorized workshops are 30-50% higher: Rs 400-600/hour vs Rs 250-400 at independents. Independent workshops offer flexibility and often faster turnaround. For critical components (engine, transmission, brakes), authorized service maintains warranty. Routine maintenance (oil, filters, minor repairs) can go to trusted independents. Labor rates vary by region: metros Rs 400-600, tier-2 Rs 300-450, tier-3 Rs 200-350. Build relationships with 1-2 workshops for consistent quality and negotiated rates.

chunks.title: OEM vs Aftermarket Parts Comparison
chunks.content: Parts sourcing significantly impacts cost and reliability. OEM (Original Equipment Manufacturer) parts ensure fit, performance, and often preserve warranty—cost 40-80% more than aftermarket. Aftermarket parts from reputed brands (SPM, IPF, Rane) offer 30-50% savings with acceptable quality for non-critical components. Avoid cheap unbranded parts for safety-critical items: brakes, steering, suspension. Engine and transmission components—prefer OEM during warranty. Filters, belts, and routine items—quality aftermarket is acceptable. Document parts used for warranty claims and resale value. Counterfeit parts are prevalent—source from authorized distributors only.

chunks.title: Breakdown Prevention: Common Failure Points
chunks.content: Understanding common failure points enables proactive prevention. Battery failure is the top cause of roadside breakdowns—test every 6 months, replace at 2-3 years. Fuel system issues (clogged filters, injector failure) cause 15-20% of breakdowns. Overheating from coolant leaks or radiator issues—inspect hoses and coolant level regularly. Brake failures often stem from worn shoes or air system leaks. Clutch failures typically give warning through slipping. Tyre blowouts from under-inflation or overload. Electrical faults in alternator or wiring. Pre-trip inspections catch 60-70% of potential failures. Carry essential spares: fan belt, fuses, bulbs, and tools.

chunks.title: Maintenance Record Keeping Best Practices
chunks.content: Comprehensive maintenance records support fleet management and vehicle valuation. Record every service: date, odometer, work performed, parts used, cost, and workshop. Use digital fleet management systems for centralized records accessible to operations. Track cost per km for each vehicle to identify high-maintenance assets. Records support warranty claims—OEMs require proof of authorized service. At resale, documented maintenance history can add 5-10% to vehicle value. Schedule reminders based on km or time—whichever comes first. Analyze trends: rising costs may indicate replacement timing. Maintain records for minimum 5 years for compliance and reference.

chunks.title: Warranty Management for New Commercial Vehicles
chunks.content: New truck warranties typically cover 2-3 years or 3-5 lakh kilometres, whichever comes first. Warranty requires service at authorized workshops using OEM parts at specified intervals. Missing even one scheduled service can void warranty—maintain strict compliance. Extended warranty packages are available from OEMs for 1-2 additional years. Document all warranty work for potential goodwill claims. Know warranty exclusions: wear items (tyres, brake pads), negligence, modifications. Transfer warranty to second owner if selling—some OEMs allow this. Warranty period is the best time to identify manufacturing defects—report issues promptly.

chunks.title: Battery Maintenance for Fleet Vehicles
chunks.content: Battery failure is a leading cause of fleet downtime. Indian fleet vehicles typically use Exide or Amaron batteries costing Rs 8,000-15,000 depending on capacity. Expected life is 2-3 years with proper maintenance. Check electrolyte levels monthly in non-sealed batteries. Clean terminals to prevent corrosion—apply petroleum jelly. Test voltage and load capacity every 6 months; replace if below 12.4V when charged. Ensure alternator charges properly—undercharging shortens battery life. Extreme heat accelerates degradation—park in shade when possible. Keep spare battery for critical vehicles. Consider AMF (Automatic Mains Failure) batteries for refrigerated trucks.

chunks.title: Cooling System Maintenance
chunks.content: Overheating causes severe engine damage—cooling system maintenance is critical. Check coolant level weekly; top up with correct coolant mix (not plain water in summer). Flush and replace coolant every 50,000 km or 2 years. Inspect hoses for cracks, softness, or leaks at each service. Radiator cap maintains pressure—replace if not holding. Thermostat failure causes overheating; replace every 80,000-100,000 km. Clean radiator fins of debris—clogged radiators reduce efficiency 30-40%. Water pump bearings wear out—listen for whining. In Indian heat, consider higher-capacity radiators for heavy loads. Overheating can cause head gasket failure costing Rs 30,000-80,000.

chunks.title: Pre-Trip and Post-Trip Inspection Checklist
chunks.content: Driver-conducted inspections prevent roadside failures and ensure safety. Pre-trip: check oil, coolant, brake fluid levels; inspect tyres for pressure, cuts, wear; test lights, horn, wipers; verify brake operation; check mirrors and reflectors; ensure documents (RC, insurance, permit) are current. Post-trip: report any unusual sounds, vibrations, or warning lights; note fluid leaks; document odometer. Use standardized checklist—digital apps ensure nothing is missed. Defects found should ground vehicle until repaired. Training drivers on inspection importance improves compliance. Failed inspections should trigger maintenance workflow. Document inspection results for compliance and trend analysis.
